gloss: A small, narrow boat with pointed ends, propelled by paddles; used by many Indigenous peoples across North America
A small, narrow boat with a covered deck and one or two cockpits; originally developed by Arctic peoples
A dome-shaped shelter built from blocks of snow, traditionally used by Inuit peoples in winter; provides effective insulation through the catenary arch structure
A concentrated mixture of lean dried meat, rendered fat, and dried berries, traditional to Plains Cree, Métis, and other First Nations. Heritage domain extension: not in standard WordNet.
The art of decorating objects using dyed porcupine quills, a traditional craft of many First Nations peoples. Heritage domain extension: not in standard WordNet.
The bark of the birch tree, used by Anishinaabe and other First Nations peoples to construct canoes, containers, and structures
An Inuit oil lamp carved from soapstone, used for light, heat, and cooking. Central to Inuit women's knowledge tradition. Heritage domain extension: not in standard WordNet.
A bowed string instrument central to Appalachian and Métis music traditions; the Métis fiddle tradition blends European and Indigenous musical forms
A device for weaving cloth; traditional looms for weaving wool, plant fiber, and sinew appear across Appalachian, First Nations, and Inuit traditions
A furnace or oven used to harden clay pottery; traditional pottery firing methods include pit firing, bonfire firing, and wood-fired kilns
A furnace where metal is heated for shaping; the Appalachian blacksmith forge is central to traditional metalworking in highland communities
Wild leeks (Allium tricoccum) harvested in early spring in Appalachian forests; a highly valued traditional food and the subject of spring foraging festivals
Tendon from caribou, moose, or other animals, processed and used as thread; traditionally used by Inuit and First Nations for sewing clothing and watercraft
A large deer of arctic and subarctic regions, central to Inuit material culture; caribou provides food, clothing, sinew, bone tools, and fat for lamp fuel
An Inuit dog sled constructed from wood or bone and lashed with sinew, designed for travel across sea ice and tundra. Heritage domain extension: not in standard WordNet.
Hierochloe odorata, a fragrant grass used in basket weaving and ceremony by many First Nations peoples; its harvesting is governed by protocols ensuring sustainability
An edible wild mushroom prized in Appalachian foraging tradition; safe identification requires distinguishing from toxic false morels
The process of preserving food in sealed jars by heat processing; water-bath canning is safe for high-acid foods only — low-acid foods require pressure canning
Food preservation by exposure to wood smoke; used across Appalachian, First Nations, and Inuit traditions for fish, meat, and berries; requires minimum internal temperatures for safety
A large, flat frame attached to the foot for walking on snow without sinking; designs vary by nation and ecological region — Dene, Cree, and Anishinaabe traditions each have distinct designs
